g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on the sigaction() function in UNIX/Linux signal handling.
Signals are software interrupts that notify processes of events like Ctrl+C presses or timer expirations.
The sigaction() function provides robust, portable signal handling with finer control than the older signal() function.
g.text: • More robust and portable than signal()
• Finer control over signal handling behavior
• Specify if system calls should restart automatically

g.data-narration: The struct sigaction has four key fields that define how signals are handled in your program.
The sa_handler points to your signal handler function, while sa_sigaction provides extended information when SA_SIGINFO is set.
g.text: SIG_DFL = default action | SIG_IGN = ignore signal
Used when SA_SIGINFO flag is set

g.data-narration: The sa_mask field specifies which signals to block during handler execution using sigset_t operations.
g.text: Signal sets are manipulated using these functions:
Example: sigemptyset(&sa.sa_mask); // Initialize to empty set

g.data-narration: The sa_flags field modifies signal handling behavior with several important flags like SA_RESTART and SA_SIGINFO.
g.text: Also: SA_ONSTACK - use alternate signal stack

g.data-narration: The SA_RESTART flag automatically restarts interrupted system calls, avoiding the need for manual EINTR handling.
g.text: SA_RESTART Flag - Automatic System Call Restart
System calls fail with errno = EINTR
Note: Not all syscalls are restartable (e.g., select, poll may still fail)

g.data-narration: When SA_SIGINFO is set, the handler receives a siginfo_t structure with detailed information about the signal source.
g.text: Handler signature: void handler(int signum, siginfo_t *info, void *context)

g.data-narration: The sigaction() function takes three parameters: the signal number, new action, and optional old action storage.
g.text: int sigaction(int signum, const struct sigaction *act, struct sigaction *oldact);

g.data-narration: sigaction() is preferred over signal() because it's portable, supports SA_RESTART, and allows retrieving previous handlers.
g.text: signal() vs sigaction() - Why sigaction() is Preferred
Portable across POSIX systems, supports SA_RESTART
✓ Portable | ✓ Signal mask during handler | ✓ SA_RESTART | ✓ Retrieve old action

g.data-narration: This example shows how to set up a basic signal handler for SIGINT using sigaction().
This example demonstrates using SA_SIGINFO to get detailed information about the sending process.
g.text: Example 2: Using SA_SIGINFO for Extended Information

g.data-narration: This example shows blocking signals during handler execution and automatic syscall restart.
g.text: sigaction(SIGUSR1, &sa, &old_sa); // Block SIGTERM during SIGUSR1 handling

g.data-narration: Important notes: SIGKILL and SIGSTOP cannot be caught or ignored. Always initialize sa_mask with sigemptyset().
g.text: SIGKILL & SIGSTOP: Cannot be caught, blocked, or ignored
Use async-signal-safe functions in handlers (e.g., write(), not printf())

g.data-narration: Key exam tips: Remember the four sigaction fields, understand SA_RESTART behavior, and know siginfo_t fields.
g.text: SA_NOCLDSTOP: Don't generate SIGCHLD when child stops (only on terminate)

g.data-narration: To summarize: sigaction() provides portable, robust signal handling with fine control over signal masks, flags, and handler behavior.
g.text: sigaction() = Portable, controlled signal handling | Use SA_RESTART for syscalls | SA_SIGINFO for extended info

g.data-narration: Quick reference: sigaction() returns 0 on success, -1 on error. Use sigemptyset() to initialize masks.
g.text: Thank you for watching! Practice writing signal handlers to master this topic.
